title: Four-shaft twill scarf
subtitle: Weaving a 25 x 160 cm scarf on a four-shaft loom with a straight 2/2 twill threading
excerpt: A 2/2 twill on four shafts produces the diagonal-line structure found in denim, tweed, and herringbone. The threading is a straight 1-2-3-4 sequence and the treadling simple: four sheds in order, repeating. This scarf uses a two-colour alternating warp against a single weft to demonstrate how warp and weft colours shift in proportion as the twill diagonal builds across the cloth.
sourceNotes: 2/2 twill on four shafts is the entry-point multi-shaft structure. Threading and treadling sequences are standard loom-weaving practice consistent with contemporary handweaving instruction.

content.text: threads each warp end in the straight sequence 1-2-3-4 and treads in the same order: shafts 1+2, shafts 2+3, shafts 3+4, shafts 4+1. A
of 8 ends per cm suits DK-weight wool in a 2/2 twill structure.

items.name: Four-shaft floor loom or table loom
Warp yarn colour B (DK wool, contrasting)

content.text: Warp the loom to a 25 cm weaving width at 8 ends per cm, alternating colours A and B across the full width. Thread each end in the straight sequence: shaft 1, shaft 2, shaft 3, shaft 4, repeat. Sley the reed at 2 ends per dent in a 10-dent reed, or 1 end per dent in an 8-dent reed. Tie on with consistent tension across all warp groups.

The four-pick treadling sequence is: raise shafts 1 and 2 together (pick 1), raise shafts 2 and 3 (pick 2), raise shafts 3 and 4 (pick 3), raise shafts 4 and 1 (pick 4). Pass the weft after each shed. Beat firmly and consistently: twill requires a slightly firmer beat than tabby to produce a balanced, even cloth.

After 10 picks, look at the diagonal forming on the surface. The warp floats should step one thread to the right with each pick, producing a clear 45-degree diagonal. If the diagonal is unclear or broken, check that the correct pair of shafts is raised in sequence and that no shed was repeated or skipped.

Cut the scarf from the loom with 20 cm of warp fringe at each end. Knot the fringe in pairs close to the fell line. Wet-finish the scarf by hand-washing gently in cool water, roll it in a towel to remove excess water, then press flat with a steam iron while still slightly damp. Wet-finishing opens the cloth structure and allows the wool fibres to bloom into a fuller, softer hand.

items.fix: Mark each pick on a small tally as you weave. If a break appears, count back to the last correct row and unweave to that point.
items.cause: A shed in the treadling sequence was skipped or repeated
items.problem: The diagonal line is broken or reverses direction partway through

items.fix: Twill should be sett at approximately two-thirds of the tabby sett for the same yarn. After wet-finishing, if the cloth is still stiff, open the sett on the next warp.
items.cause: Sett too dense for the yarn, or beat too heavy
items.problem: The cloth is stiff and board-like rather than drapey

items.fix: Wind colours A and B together on the warping board. Treat each pair as one end. That keeps both colours in equal proportion.
items.cause: Colours wound in unequal proportions on the warping board
items.problem: The two warp colour stripes are uneven in width
